Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

Part 1 · Geodesy
  • 01Define and explain why geodesy is needed in GIS.
  • 02Differentiate the three key representations of Earth’s surface.
  • 03Explain the difference between a horizontal and a vertical datum.
Part 2 · Projections
  • 04Explain what a map projection does.
  • 05Describe the three core ingredients of projections (type, aspect, tangency).
  • 06Explain why distortion is unavoidable and how it varies across a map.
  • 07Choose and justify a projection goal — conformal, equal-area, equidistant, or azimuthal — from a map’s purpose.
Part 1 · Geodesy

Two kinds of coordinate systems

Recall the two ways to pin down a location:

  • φλA spherical (geographic) coordinate system measures position with latitude and longitude on the surface of a globe.
  • xyA 2-D Cartesian coordinate system measures position with x/y in linear units (meters, feet) from an origin (0, 0).
2-D Cartesian coordinate systems are created via map projections.
A map projection is the systematic mathematical rendering of latitude/longitude from a 3-D spherical system to a 2-D Cartesian one.

But to build these coordinate systems, we first have to measure the Earth.

Geodesy & the ellipsoid

Geodesy is the science of measuring and modeling Earth’s size, shape, and gravity field to support accurate mapping and positioning.

The trouble: Earth is lumpy — mountains, trenches — and hard to measure exactly.

We need a simple surface to define latitude/longitude, compute approximate distances and bearings, and serve as the geometric backbone of a datum and map projections.

The ellipsoid

We often call Earth a sphere, but it’s better approximated by an ellipsoid: a smooth, mathematically-defined surface, slightly flattened at the poles.

In a GIS, the chosen ellipsoid and datum show up in the layer’s coordinate system details — here, WGS 1984 versus the older Clarke 1866 spheroid and NAD 1927 datum:

Different surveys produced different best-fit ellipsoids over time — modern GIS most often uses WGS 84:

EllipsoidSemi-major axisSemi-minor axisInverse flattening
Bessel (1841)6,377,483.865 m6,356,079.0 m1 / 299.153
Clarke (1866)6,378,206.4 m6,356,584 m1 / 294.98
Krasovsky (1940)6,378,245 m6,356,863.05 m1 / 298.3
Australian (1960)6,378,160 m6,356,774.7 m1 / 298.25
WGS (1984)6,378,137 m6,356,752.314 m1 / 298.257

Source: A Primer of GIS by Francis Harvey.

From a shape to a datum

An ellipsoid gives us a smooth shape — but a shape alone can’t tell you where anything is. To turn it into a usable reference, we lay a latitude/longitude net on top of the ellipsoid and lock it to the Earth. That alignment is a datum.

A horizontal datum defines horizontal positions (lat/long or x/y) based on an ellipsoid and its alignment to Earth.

Before plate tectonics, we assumed continents were stationary and fixed a lat/long net for all time, with zero longitude through Greenwich. But points actually drift over time as plates move:

So modern datums rely on a global measurement infrastructure that tracks point locations and tectonic shifts over time:

  • VLBIVery Long Baseline Interferometry
  • SLRSatellite Laser Ranging
  • DORISDoppler Orbitography by Radiopositioning Integrated by Satellite
  • GNSSGlobal Navigation Satellite Systems (e.g. GPS)

Geoids: measuring heights

A geoid is a gravity-related vertical reference, used as the starting point for measuring heights. The world’s oceans — our historical height reference — roughly follow this gravitationally defined surface.

Because we have two reference surfaces — geoid and ellipsoid — we get two kinds of heights. Elevation is the distance above the geoid, loosely called “height above mean sea level,” more precisely orthometric height.

Countries build a “best-fit” geoid by combining gravimeter measurements with many surveyed benchmarks. Because the data and networks differ by place, geoid models differ too.

When a geoid is adopted as the reference system for heights, that becomes the vertical datum.
Part 2 · Projections

Review: latitude & longitude

Latitude and longitude are angular measures. The graticule is their grid of lines.

Zero longitude is marked in Greenwich, England.

Spacing: parallels vs. meridians

A projection flattens the Earth

The best model is a globe — but we need flat maps.

A map projection is the systematic mathematical representation of latitude/longitude from a 3-D spherical system to a 2-D Cartesian one. Flattening always forces some compromise, which we’ll see next.

Type, aspect & tangency

Traditional projections combine three choices. Mix and match across these columns:

Projection type (surface)Orientation / aspectPoints of tangency
CylindricalNormalTangent
PlanarTransverseSecant
ConicOblique—

Most projections use a developable surface — a cone, cylinder, or plane you can unroll flat without stretching.

Aspect

Aspect is the orientation of the developable surface relative to the Earth. The choice shifts where distortion is smallest.

Points of tangency

Where the surface touches the globe are the standard parallels or standard lines. In traditional projections, those contact lines have the least distortion.

Traditional projection types

A few classics built on developable surfaces:

Not every projection uses a developable surface. Pseudocylindrical projections — Mollweide, sinusoidal, Goode homolosine — project directly from an ellipsoid using a formula. You can spot them: straight parallels and a straight central meridian, but all other meridians curve.

It’s all equations

Traditional or not, every projection is ultimately an equation that translates a (latitude, longitude) pair into an (x, y) position on the flat page. Pick or design that equation well and you get a useful map. Get it “wrong” — or just experiment — and you get one of Andy Woodruff’s delightful accidental map projections.

Distortion is unavoidable

You can’t go from 3-D to 2-D without distorting shape, area, distance, or direction. Each projection preserves one or two of these and sacrifices the rest — a tradeoff chosen for the map’s purpose.

PreservesWhat & the tradeoffUse caseExample
Shape (conformal)Local angles are preserved, so shapes look right. Tradeoff: area and distance distort.Navigation, meteorology — bearings matter.Mercator, Lambert Conformal Conic.
Area (equal-area)A region twice as big on Earth is twice as big on the map. Tradeoff: shapes get squished, especially far from standard lines.Comparing “how much” per area (population, land cover, disease).Albers Equal-Area Conic.
Distance (equidistant)Distances are true, but only in specific ways (from one point or along certain lines). Tradeoff: other distances, shape, area.Distance from a central point, routing, shortest path.Azimuthal Equidistant.
Direction (azimuthal)Directions are true from a central point. Tradeoff: other directions, distances, shape, area.Which way to go from a central point.Azimuthal projections.
Group brainstorm
You’re mapping disease rates per county and want a fair comparison of “how much.” Which property must the projection preserve?
Answer Area (equal-area). If areas aren’t preserved, a rate normalized by area is misleading — big regions look more (or less) important than they are.
Part 3 · ArcGIS Pro demo

Tissot’s indicatrix

Tissot’s indicatrix is a visual aid for seeing projection distortion, from 19th-century mathematician Nicolas Tissot. The idea: any small circle on the globe, projected onto the flat map, reveals that location’s distortion through its projected shape and size.

Activity · discussion board

Build a Tissot map in ArcGIS Pro

Pick a projection and write a short paragraph: its type, what it preserves, what it distorts, and what it’s used for. In ArcGIS Pro, make a map showing Tissot’s ellipses over the landmass and paste it under your paragraph. Use the example below as a template, then submit to the course discussion board.

Example · Plate Carrée (by Taylor Anderson) The figure shows the plate carrée projection, a cylindrical projection. It is equidistant along any meridian and the equator. Shape, scale, and area distortion increase with distance from the equator. North/south/east/west directions are always accurate, but general directions distort except locally along the equator. It’s used for simple portrayals of the world or regions with minimal data and no need for accurate areas — handy for index maps and for mapping phenomena that change with longitude, such as time zones.

Key takeaways

1
Two ways to describe location: lat/long on a globe (spherical) vs x/y in meters/feet (Cartesian).
2
Map projections are the math that converts 3-D lat/long into 2-D x/y so we can make flat maps.
3
We use simplified surfaces: an ellipsoid backs the horizontal datum; a geoid is the gravity-based surface for heights — once adopted, it’s the vertical datum.
4
Most traditional projections come down to three levers: type, aspect, and tangency.
5
Distortion is inevitable — smallest near standard lines, larger farther away.
6
There’s no universally “best” projection — choose by what you must preserve: shape, area, distance, or direction.

Key terms & concepts

Open any term to see its definition and an example.
